rationale_for_baseline_tier: Acute limb ischaemia with an immediately-threatened limb (Rutherford IIb — sensory loss plus motor weakness) from a cardiogenic embolus, with a salvage window of only ~4–6 hours before irreversible necrosis and amputation. The patient lives alone and cannot drive, and the embolic source can shower to the brain, heart or other limbs. This warrants T5 — call 000 for an emergency (Priority-1) ambulance to a vascular-surgery-capable centre; self-transport is neither safe nor feasible.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: A suddenly cold, pale/mottled, numb or weak hand or arm (the picture in this case)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — every minute of delay narrows the ~4–6 hour limb-salvage window
escalation_edges.rationale: A pulseless, cold, mottled limb with sensorimotor loss is an acute arterial occlusion — an emergency needing revascularisation within hours to avoid amputation.
escalation_edges.communication_template: This is an emergency — I'm asking you to call 000 for an ambulance right now and tell them your arm has suddenly gone cold, pale and numb. You need a hospital with vascular surgeons as fast as possible.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New chest pain, facial droop, slurred speech, or the same cold/pale change in another limb
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — tell the 000 operator if this develops before or during transport
escalation_edges.rationale: The atrial clot source can embolise elsewhere — to the coronary, cerebral or mesenteric circulation or the other limb; these are additional life-threatening emergencies.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get chest pain, your face droops, your speech slurs, or your other arm or leg goes cold and pale — tell the 000 operator straight away, because the clot can travel to other arteries.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Complete loss of movement and sensation, or skin turning fixed blue/black and not blanching
escalation_edges.rationale: Fixed mottling and complete sensorimotor loss mark progression toward irreversibility (Rutherford III); the ambulance must be expedited and the receiving team pre-alerted.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the hand becomes completely numb and you can't move it at all, or the skin goes a fixed blue-black colour, make sure 000 knows immediately — this means the tissue is being lost and speed is everything.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone and cannot drive because of the pain and loss of arm function
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Self-transport is not an option — an emergency ambulance (000) is required, not a suggestion to get to hospital independently. Advise keeping the front door unlocked and, if possible, a phone within reach so paramedics can get in.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Atrial fibrillation with three days of missed anticoagulation
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Communicate the ongoing embolic-shower risk to the patient and in the 000 handover; do not advise a self-administered anticoagulant dose before possible emergency surgery.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 for an ambulance right now and tell them your arm has suddenly become cold, pale and numb and that you have an irregular heartbeat. While you wait, keep the arm hanging down by your side, below heart level — do NOT raise it. Do NOT put a heat pack or ice on it, because the numb skin can be burned without you feeling it. Don't eat or drink anything in case you need an operation. Stay seated, keep your keys in your good hand and unlock the front door for the paramedics.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Call 000 immediately for an emergency ambulance
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 operates 24 hours a day — this is a limb-threatening emergency that cannot wait for clinic hours or a routine appointment.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: The hospital will provide a discharge summary covering the revascularisation and the anticoagulation plan; the GP should ensure the anticoagulant is re-established and never interrupted, and follow up AF management and a reliable repeat-prescription process.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating a limb-threatening acute arterial occlusion as a musculoskeletal/neurological complaint or a DVT and routing to self-care, a GP appointment or same-day urgent care instead of 000.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: The 4–6 hour salvage window closes, the limb infarcts, and the patient loses the arm to amputation; an embolic shower could also cause a stroke or MI.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Recognising an emergency but advising the patient to self-transport to the ED when they live alone and cannot drive, risking a delay that self-transport cannot safely achieve and eroding the salvage window.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Pulses, temperature demarcation and imaging cannot be confirmed remotely, so the exact level and reversibility of the occlusion are unknown via telehealth.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat the reported 6 Ps as a limb-threatening arterial emergency and escalate to 000 immediately — uncertainty about the level does not change the need for emergency revascularisation.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Using the inability to examine as a reason to temporise — arranging remote analgesia, outpatient imaging or a GP review — which wastes the salvage window.
